label: Coreference Resolution
definition: The task of finding which expressions in a text refer to the same entity and grouping them into chains, linking "she" and "it" back to the right antecedents. It is a sensitive probe of genuine understanding because the hardest cases, the Winograd sentences where "it was too big" must bind to the trophy not the suitcase, turn on commonsense reasoning rather than grammar.

body.text: Coreference resolution finds all the expressions in a document that point to the same entity and groups them together. In "Marie won the prize because she had worked for years on it", a system must recognize that "she" refers to "Marie" and "it" refers to "the prize", and each such group of co-referring mentions is a coreference chain. The problem is fundamental to genuine language understanding, because human text is saturated with pronouns, abbreviations, and repeated descriptions that only make sense once the reader knows who or what each one denotes.

The task breaks into recognizable subproblems. The most familiar is pronoun resolution, also called anaphora resolution, where a pronoun must be linked back to an earlier antecedent, but coreference is broader: it also links full noun phrases, as when "the president", "Lincoln", and "he" all refer to one person across a paragraph. Resolving these links draws on many signals at once: grammatical agreement in number and gender narrows the candidates, syntactic structure from dependency parsing constrains what a pronoun can bind to, the entity types from named-entity recognition rule out mismatches, and world knowledge often settles the rest.

That last signal is what makes the task hard, sharpest in the famous Winograd cases: "the trophy did not fit in the suitcase because it was too big" requires reasoning to know "it" is the trophy, since flipping "big" to "small" flips the referent with no change in grammar. These sentences were designed precisely to resist any purely syntactic shortcut, which is why they became a benchmark for commonsense rather than parsing.

Approaches to coreference have tracked the broader history of NLP. Early systems were rule-based, encoding linguistic constraints and salience heuristics by hand; these gave way to statistical models that scored candidate antecedents with learned features, and then to neural models. Modern systems are typically built on contextual encoders like bert, where self-attention already produces representations sensitive to which earlier words a token relates to, making the model well suited to scoring which mention a given span corefers with, and some recent work casts coreference as a sequence-to-sequence problem, having a model annotate the text with its entity links directly.

Coreference resolution matters because so many downstream tasks silently depend on it. Information extraction must attribute every fact to the right entity, summarization must not lose track of who did what, question answering must follow pronouns to their referents, and machine translation must carry agreement across a passage. It is also a sensitive probe of how well a model truly understands a text rather than merely processing its surface, which is why coreference benchmarks remain a respected measure of language understanding and why the hardest cases continue to challenge even strong large models.

image.alt: In the text Maria found a book, She read it, an arrow links the pronoun she back to its antecedent Maria; labeled pronoun and antecedent.
image.caption: Coreference resolution is the task of figuring out which words refer to the same entity, linking a pronoun like she or it back to the noun it stands for. It is essential for any real understanding of a passage and surprisingly hard, because the right antecedent often depends on world knowledge rather than grammar alone.
